editableNewDialogue.text: I’m working on idioms. Can you give me three short sentences using common idioms, and then explain what each idiom means?

Sure—here are three examples, with meanings:

1) “When the Wi‑Fi stopped working five minutes before the meeting, it was the last straw.”
   • Idiom: “the last straw” — the final problem in a series that makes you feel you can’t tolerate it anymore.

2) “I didn’t want to guess, so I asked a friend to give me the gist of the article.”
   • Idiom: “the gist” — the main idea or most important points.

3) “The coach told the team to get their act together before the tournament.”
   • Idiom: “get your act together” — to become organized and start doing things more effectively.

If you want, I can rewrite each sentence to sound more formal or more casual.
Can you make them a bit easier and more daily-life?

Absolutely:

1) “I spilled coffee on my shirt again—this is the last straw.”
   • “the last straw” = the final annoyance that makes you upset.

2) “I didn’t read the whole message—just tell me the gist.”
   • “the gist” = the main point.

3) “My room is a mess. I need to get my act together.”
   • “get your act together” = get organized and behave more responsibly.
Nice. One more: give me an idiom that means “do something quickly,” and show two examples.

Idiom: “in a flash” — very quickly.

Examples:
1) “I’ll be back in a flash—I just need to grab my keys.”
2) “The ice cream melted in a flash because it was so hot outside.”

(Extra note not about you: In a 1960s space-race anecdote, engineers joked that some calculations had to be done “in a flash” because launch windows were so short—whether or not the story is perfectly true, the phrase fits that kind of situation.)

Could you rewrite this in simple words again, but keep the same meaning? I’m trying to make it sound more natural for Part 2, and I always run out of time when I speak.

“Last week I tried to practice the story about finding a student ID card in the library, but when I talked, I stopped many times and couldn’t finish in one minute. I want it to be smoother and easier to remember.”

Sure. Here’s a simpler, more natural version:

“Last week, I was in the library doing my homework when I saw a student ID card on the table next to me. No one was sitting there, so I waited for a while, hoping the owner would come back. But nobody came.

So I decided to help. I posted a short message online and left my phone number. About an hour later, the owner called me, and I gave the card back to her. She thanked me a lot.

I felt really happy because it was a small thing for me, but it helped someone else. It also made me want to help more people in the future.”
By the way, I need to correct something from before. My long-term goal for IELTS is actually **7.0**, not 6.5. My university updated the requirement, so I have to aim higher now.
Got it—so your long-term goal has changed. Since your university updated the requirement, your target is **an overall IELTS 7.0**, rather than 6.5.
Yes, exactly—**IELTS 7.0** is the score I’m working toward.

annotation.queryText: I’m planning my next few months of study and need to pick a realistic benchmark to aim for on that big English proficiency test so I can decide whether to book an exam slot soon or keep preparing longer. What specific score target should I use as my benchmark based on what I’ve told you before?
